```text
+package spi
+
+import (
+	"errors"

+
+	"github.com/specstoryai/getspecstory/specstory-cli/pkg/spi/schema"
+)
+
+// ErrReconstructionUnsupported is returned by a provider that carries the
+// ReconstructSession responsibility but does not yet have a native serializer.
+// Reconstruction is on the Provider interface so every provider implements the
+// method, but only some providers (initially Claude Code and Codex CLI) produce
+// real native output.
+var ErrReconstructionUnsupported = errors.New("session reconstruction not supported by this provider")
+
+// ReconstructOptions controls how a session is reconstructed into native form.
+type ReconstructOptions struct {
+	// WorkspaceRoot is the target working directory the reconstructed session
+	// should belong to. Native paths/IDs are derived from it. When empty, the
+	// provider falls back to the SessionData's own WorkspaceRoot.
+	WorkspaceRoot string
+
+	// MigrationNote, when set, is prepended as a leading agent turn so the agent
+	// understands the prior turns were imported from another session. Optional.
+	MigrationNote string
+}
+
+// ReconstructedSession is the native output of reconstruction. It is data only;
+// the caller decides where (and whether) to write it.
+type ReconstructedSession struct {
+	SessionID string // freshly minted, native-format session ID
+	Filename  string // suggested native filename (base name, not a full path)
+	Content   []byte // native session file bytes
+}
+
+// Turn is a single role-tagged text turn in the flattened transcript. The
+// reconstruction model collapses everything an agent said, thought, or did into
+// plain user/agent text turns (see docs/SESSION-PORTABILITY.md), which each
+// provider then serializes into its native format.
+type Turn struct {
+	Role string // schema.RoleUser or schema.RoleAgent
+	Text string
+}
+
...
```

```text
43	+// Turn is a single role-tagged text turn in the flattened transcript. The

47	+type Turn struct {
48	+	Role string // schema.RoleUser or schema.RoleAgent
49	+	Text string
50	+}
51	+
52	+// FlattenSessionData reduces a SessionData into an ordered list of plain
53	+// user/agent text turns. This is the single place the cross-provider flattening
54	+// policy lives, so every provider's serializer consumes the same transcript.
55	+//
56	+// Rules (per the locked design):
57	+//   - user/agent text content -> a turn of that role
58	+//   - thinking content -> folded into the agent turn's text (not dropped)
59	+//   - tool calls -> folded into the agent turn's text via the pre-rendered
60	+//     Summary / FormattedMarkdown captured on the forward pass
61	+//   - model, usage, and path hints -> dropped
62	+//
63	+// migrationNote, when non-empty, becomes a leading agent turn.
64	+func FlattenSessionData(data *schema.SessionData, migrationNote string) []Turn {
65	+	var turns []Turn
66	+
67	+	if strings.TrimSpace(migrationNote) != "" {
68	+		turns = append(turns, Turn{Role: schema.RoleAgent, Text: strings.TrimSpace(migrationNote)})
69	+	}
70	+
71	+	if data == nil {
72	+		return turns
73	+	}
74	+
75	+	for i := range data.Exchanges {
76	+		for j := range data.Exchanges[i].Messages {
77	+			msg := &data.Exchanges[i].Messages[j]
78	+
79	+			// Only user and agent roles produce turns; anything else is skipped.
80	+			if msg.Role != schema.RoleUser && msg.Role != schema.RoleAgent {
81	+				continue
82	+			}
83	+
84	+			text := messageTurnText(msg)
85	+			if strings.TrimSpace(text) == "" {
86	+				continue
87	+			}
88	+
89	+			// Drop synthetic, non-conversational turns (slash-command invocations,
90	+			// local-command output, etc.) so they are not replayed into the resumed
91	+			// session. This is intentionally reconstruction-only — archival markdown
92	+			// stays faithful to the source session.
93	+			if isSyntheticTurnText(text) {
94	+				continue
95	+			}
96	+
97	+			turns = append(turns, Turn{Role: msg.Role, Text: text})
98	+		}
99	+	}
100	+
101	+	return turns
102	+}
103	+
104	+// syntheticTurnMarkers are wrapper tags agents inject for non-conversational
105	+// records — slash-command invocations, their stdout, and guard/caveat notes (e.g.
106	+// Claude Code's <command-name>/<local-command-*> and <TEXTBLOCK> title-generation
107	+// prompts). These are noise — and sometimes actively misleading — when replayed
108	+// into a resumed session, so reconstruction drops any turn whose text contains
109	+// one. Markdown generation does NOT use this filter and stays faithful to source.
110	+var syntheticTurnMarkers = []string{
111	+	"<command-name>",
112	+	"<command-message>",
113	+	"<command-args>",
114	+	"<local-command-stdout>",
115	+	"<local-command-caveat>",
116	+	"<TEXTBLOCK>",
117	+}
118	+
119	+// isSyntheticTurnText reports whether a flattened turn is a synthetic,
120	+// non-conversational artifact that should not be replayed into a resumed session.
121	+func isSyntheticTurnText(text string) bool {
122	+	for _, marker := range syntheticTurnMarkers {
123	+		if strings.Contains(text, marker) {
124	+			return true
125	+		}
126	+	}
127	+	return false
128	+}
129	BODY:
130	isSyntheticTurnText drops any turn whose text *contains* a marker string. This is likely too aggressive: if a user/agent message mentions one of these tags mid-sentence (or in pasted code), the entire turn will be dropped from reconstructed sessions. Using a trimmed prefix check is less error-prone and matches the intent of filtering wrapper records rather than arbitrary content.

192	+
193	+'search' opens an interactive search of your session history. Type to search, press space to

208	+				return err
209	+			}

211	+
212	+			if total, _ := store.Count(); total == 0 {
213	+				fprintln(os.Stderr, "\nNo agent sessions indexed yet. Run an agent, then try again (or `specstory reindex`).")
214	+				return nil
215	+			}
216	+
217	+			homeID, homeName, idErr := utils.ComputeProjectID(cwd)
218	+			if idErr != nil || homeID == "" {

222	+

270	+				return fmt.Errorf("unknown target agent %q: %w", rm.result.targetID, err)
271	+			}
272	+			_ = config.SaveResumePrefs(viewMode, rm.result.targetID)

274	search saves resume prefs using the *initial* viewMode loaded from config, not the view mode the user may have toggled during the TUI session (rm.viewMode). This can overwrite the user's latest dense/sparse choice when exiting `specstory search`.

281	@@ -186,8 +181,30 @@ func (m *ProjectIdentityManager) generateWorkspaceID() string {
282	 	}
283	 
284	 	// The TypeScript version hashes the workspace URI, but in our case
285	-	// we'll hash the absolute path for compatibility
286	-	return m.createHash(absPath)
287	+	// we'll hash the absolute path for compatibility. The path is canonicalized first so
288	+	// the same physical directory yields one id regardless of the casing a caller passes
289	+	// (os.Getwd may echo a lowercase $PWD; a session's recorded cwd may differ in case).
290	+	return m.createHash(canonicalizeWorkspacePath(absPath))
291	+}
292	+
293	+// caseInsensitiveFilesystem reports whether the host OS treats paths case-insensitively.
294	+// macOS (APFS/HFS+) and Windows (NTFS) are case-insensitive/case-preserving; Linux is
295	+// case-sensitive. It is a var so tests can exercise both behaviors. Windows is included
296	+// even though the app is built only for macOS/Linux today: the in-flight Windows support
297	+// lives on a branch that does not carry this file, so folding case here keeps that branch
298	BODY:
299	caseInsensitiveFilesystem is derived from runtime.GOOS, but filesystem case-sensitivity is a per-volume property on macOS (APFS can be case-sensitive). On a case-sensitive macOS volume, unconditional lowercasing will collapse distinct paths (e.g. Foo vs foo) into the same workspace_id, causing collisions / mis-attribution.

335	+// Best-effort UI writers. The destination is the user's terminal, so write
336	+// errors are not actionable and are intentionally ignored.
337	+func fprintf(w io.Writer, format string, a ...any) { _, _ = fmt.Fprintf(w, format, a...) }
338	+func fprintln(w io.Writer, a ...any)               { _, _ = fmt.Fprintln(w, a...) }
339	+func fprint(w io.Writer, a ...any)                 { _, _ = fmt.Fprint(w, a...) }
340	+
341	+// resumePlan captures the user's choices from the interactive selection.
342	+type resumePlan struct {
343	+	from      spi.Provider
344	+	fromID    string
345	+	sessionID string
346	+	// fromCwd is the directory the source session was originally launched from
347	+	// (the index's origin_cwd). It can differ from the user's current cwd when a
348	+	// session is picked from another project via the all-projects browser. The
349	+	// source data load must use this, because some providers (e.g. Codex) locate a
350	+	// session by matching its recorded cwd — looking under the current cwd would
351	+	// find nothing. Empty for older index rows; callers fall back to the current cwd.

363	+// CreateResumeCommand builds the `specstory resume` command. It opens the interactive
364	+// picker (a Bubble Tea TUI over the sessions.db index — see docs/RESUME-TUI.md): browse
365	+// the current project's sessions across all agents, pick one, choose a target agent, then
366	+// reconstruct (cross-agent) or native-resume (same agent) and launch with auto-save.
367	+func CreateResumeCommand(cloudURL *string, localTimeZone bool, debugDir string) *cobra.Command {
368	+	longDesc := `Resume a past coding-agent session — in the same agent, or a different one.
369	+
370	+'resume' opens an interactive picker of the sessions in the current project across all
371	+agents. Pick one, choose which installed agent to continue it in, and go. Resuming into a
372	+different agent reconstructs the conversation into that agent's native format first.
373	+
374	+Pass an agent to set the resume target up front, e.g. 'specstory resume codex' — the picker
375	+then skips the target-selection step and resumes straight into that agent. The agent must be
376	+a known, installed provider, or the command errors.`
377	+
378	+	resumeCmd := &cobra.Command{
379	+		Use:   "resume [agent]",
380	+		Short: "Resume a past session, optionally in a different agent",
381	+		Long:  longDesc,
382	+		Args:  cobra.MaximumNArgs(1),
383	+		RunE: func(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string) error {
384	+			config.EnsureDefaultProjectConfig()
385	+			slog.Info("Running in resume mode")
386	+
387	+			registry := factory.GetRegistry()
388	+
389	+			// Optional positional arg pre-selects the target agent (e.g. `resume codex`).
390	+			// An unknown agent name should fail the run rather than be silently ignored, so
391	+			// validate it against the registry up front. When set and valid, the picker skips
392	+			// the target-selection step entirely (see selectResumeViaTUI / beginResume); whether
393	+			// the named agent is actually installed is checked there, where the installed set
394	+			// is known.
395	+			presetTarget := ""
396	+			if len(args) == 1 {
397	+				presetTarget = strings.ToLower(strings.TrimSpace(args[0]))
398	+				if _, err := registry.Get(presetTarget); err != nil {
399	+					return utils.ValidationError{Message: fmt.Sprintf(
400	+						"unknown agent %q. Valid agents: %s", presetTarget, registry.GetProviderList())}
401	+				}
402	+			}
403	+
404	+			// Read flags (subset mirroring run/watch).
405	+			debugRaw, _ := cmd.Flags().GetBool("debug-raw")
406	+			useLocalTimezone, _ := cmd.Flags().GetBool("local-time-zone")
407	+			useUTC := !useLocalTimezone
408	+			outputDir, _ := cmd.Flags().GetString("output-dir")
409	+			flagDebugDir, _ := cmd.Flags().GetString("debug-dir")
410	+			noCloudSync, _ := cmd.Flags().GetBool("no-cloud-sync")
411	+			onlyCloudSync, _ := cmd.Flags().GetBool("only-cloud-sync")
412	+			provenanceEnabled, _ := cmd.Flags().GetBool("provenance")
413	+			noTelemetryPrompts, _ := cmd.Flags().GetBool("no-telemetry-prompts")
414	+
415	+			if flagDebugDir != "" {
416	+				spi.SetDebugBaseDir(flagDebugDir)
417	+			}
418	+
419	+			cwd, err := os.Getwd()
420	+			if err != nil {
421	+				slog.Error("Failed to get current working directory", "error", err)
422	+				return err
423	+			}
424	+
425	+			// Open (building if needed) the session index and run the interactive picker.
426	+			// builtFresh = the index was just rebuilt in the foreground, so the picker skips
427	+			// the background index warm (it would be redundant).
428	+			store, builtFresh, err := openOrBuildResumeIndex()
429	+			if err != nil {
430	+				return err
431	+			}
432	+			defer func() { _ = store.Close() }()
433	+
434	+			projectID, projectName, idErr := utils.ComputeProjectID(cwd)
435	+			if idErr != nil || projectID == "" {
436	+				projectID, projectName = unknownProjectID, filepath.Base(cwd)
437	+			}
438	+
439	+			plan, err := selectResumeViaTUI(registry, store, projectID, projectName, presetTarget, builtFresh)
440	+			if err != nil {
441	+				return err
442	+			}
443	+			if plan == nil {
444	+				// User quit or nothing to resume; the picker already explained why.
445	+				return nil
446	+			}
447	+
448	+			return launchResume(plan, cwd, resumeLaunchOpts{
449	+				outputDir:          outputDir,
450	+				flagDebugDir:       flagDebugDir,
451	+				debugRaw:           debugRaw,
452	+				useUTC:             useUTC,
453	+				noCloudSync:        noCloudSync,
454	+				onlyCloudSync:      onlyCloudSync,
455	+				provenanceEnabled:  provenanceEnabled,
456	+				noTelemetryPrompts: noTelemetryPrompts,
457	+			})
458	+		},
459	+	}
460	+
461	+	resumeCmd.Flags().String("output-dir", "", "custom output directory for markdown files (default: ./.specstory/history)")
462	+	resumeCmd.Flags().String("debug-dir", debugDir, "custom output directory for debug data (default: ./.specstory/debug)")
463	+	resumeCmd.Flags().Bool("only-cloud-sync", false, "skip local markdown file saves, only upload to cloud (requires authentication)")
464	+	resumeCmd.Flags().Bool("no-cloud-sync", false, "disable cloud sync functionality")
465	+	resumeCmd.Flags().Bool("debug-raw", false, "debug mode to output pretty-printed raw data files")
466	+	_ = resumeCmd.Flags().MarkHidden("debug-raw")
467	+	resumeCmd.Flags().Bool("local-time-zone", localTimeZone, "use local timezone for file name and content timestamps (when not present: UTC)")
468	+	resumeCmd.Flags().Bool("provenance", false, "enable AI provenance tracking (correlate file changes to agent activity)")
469	+	_ = resumeCmd.Flags().MarkHidden("provenance")
470	+	resumeCmd.Flags().StringVar(cloudURL, "cloud-url", "", "override the default cloud API base URL")
471	+	_ = resumeCmd.Flags().MarkHidden("cloud-url")
472	+	resumeCmd.Flags().Bool("no-telemetry-prompts", false, "exclude prompt text from telemetry spans, if telemetry is enabled")
473	+
474	+	return resumeCmd
475	+}
476	+
477	+// resumeLaunchOpts carries the resume launch configuration shared by `resume` and
478	+// `search` (whose `r` action resumes a found session through the same path).
479	+type resumeLaunchOpts struct {
480	+	outputDir          string
481	+	flagDebugDir       string
482	+	debugRaw           bool
483	+	useUTC             bool
484	+	noCloudSync        bool
485	+	onlyCloudSync      bool
486	+	provenanceEnabled  bool
487	+	noTelemetryPrompts bool
488	+}
489	+
490	+// launchResume reconstructs (cross-agent) or natively resumes the planned session and
491	+// runs the agent with auto-save + provenance — the shared tail of `resume` and `search`.
492	+func launchResume(plan *resumePlan, cwd string, o resumeLaunchOpts) error {
493	+	// Setup output configuration and project identity (needed for auto-save + cloud).
494	+	outConfig, err := utils.SetupOutputConfig(o.outputDir, o.flagDebugDir)
495	+	if err != nil {
496	+		return err
497	+	}
498	+	if err := utils.EnsureHistoryDirectoryExists(outConfig); err != nil {
499	+		return err
500	+	}
501	+	if _, err := utils.NewProjectIdentityManager(cwd).EnsureProjectIdentity(); err != nil {
502	+		slog.Error("Failed to ensure project identity", "error", err)
503	+	}
504	+
505	+	CheckAndWarnAuthentication(o.noCloudSync)
506	+	if o.onlyCloudSync && !cloud.IsAuthenticated() {
507	+		return utils.ValidationError{Message: "--only-cloud-sync requires authentication. Please run 'specstory login' first"}
508	+	}
509	+
510	+	analytics.SetAgentProviders([]string{plan.to.Name()})
511	+	analytics.TrackEvent(analytics.EventResumeActivated, analytics.Properties{
512	+		"from_provider": plan.fromID,
513	+		"to_provider":   plan.toID,
514	+		"cross_agent":   plan.fromID != plan.toID,
515	+	})
516	+
517	+	resumeSessionID, err := prepareResumeTarget(plan, cwd, os.Stdout)
518	+	if err != nil {
519	+		return err
520	+	}
521	+
522	+	// Context for graceful cancellation (Ctrl+C).
523	+	ctx, cancel := signal.NotifyContext(context.Background(), os.Interrupt, syscall.SIGTERM)
524	+	defer cancel()
525	+
526	+	// Provenance infrastructure (mirrors run/watch).
527	+	provenanceEngine, provenanceCleanup, err := provenance.StartEngine(o.provenanceEnabled)
528	+	if err != nil {
529	+		return err
530	+	}
531	+	defer provenanceCleanup()
532	+	fsCleanup, err := provenance.StartFSWatcher(ctx, provenanceEngine, cwd)
533	+	if err != nil {
534	+		return err
535	+	}
536	+	defer fsCleanup()
537	+
538	+	// Keep sessions.db current in real time alongside the markdown writes (nil/no-op if the
539	+	// index can't be opened — never block the resumed agent on it).
540	+	liveIndex := NewLiveIndexer(cwd)
541	+	defer liveIndex.Close()
542	+
543	+	// Auto-save callback: identical behavior to run/watch.
544	+	sessionCallback := func(sess *spi.AgentChatSession) {
545	+		if sess == nil {
546	+			return
547	+		}
548	+		_, perr := session.ProcessSingleSession(context.Background(), sess, outConfig, session.ProcessingOptions{
549	+			OnlyCloudSync:      o.onlyCloudSync,
550	+			IsAutosave:         true,
551	+			DebugRaw:           o.debugRaw,
552	+			UseUTC:             o.useUTC,
553	+			NoTelemetryPrompts: o.noTelemetryPrompts,
554	+		})
555	+		if perr != nil {
556	+			slog.Error("Failed to process session update", "sessionId", sess.SessionID, "error", perr)
557	+		}
558	+		// Mirror the markdown write into the restore index (agent we resumed INTO).
559	+		liveIndex.Record(plan.toID, sess)
560	+		provenance.ProcessEvents(ctx, provenanceEngine, sess)
561	+	}
562	+
563	+	slog.Info("Launching resume", "provider", plan.to.Name(), "resumeSessionID", resumeSessionID)
564	+	if err := plan.to.ExecAgentAndWatch(cwd, "", resumeSessionID, o.debugRaw, sessionCallback); err != nil {
565	+		slog.Error("Agent resume failed", "provider", plan.to.Name(), "error", err)
566	+		return err
567	+	}
568	+	return nil
569	+}
570	+
571	+// prepareResumeTarget produces the native session ID to hand to ExecAgentAndWatch.
572	+// Same-agent resume reuses the existing session; cross-agent reconstructs the
573	+// source SessionData into the target's native store and returns the fresh ID.
574	+func prepareResumeTarget(plan *resumePlan, cwd string, out io.Writer) (string, error) {
575	+	// Same agent: native resume on the existing session, no transform.
576	+	if plan.fromID == plan.toID {
577	+		fprintf(out, "\nResuming %s session %s in place...\n", plan.from.Name(), shortID(plan.sessionID))
578	+		return plan.sessionID, nil
579	+	}
580	+
581	+	// Cross-agent: load source SessionData, reconstruct, write into target store.
582	+	// The source must be read from the directory it was originally launched in, not the
583	+	// user's current cwd — a session picked from another project (all-projects browser)
584	+	// lives elsewhere, and providers like Codex locate it by its recorded cwd. The
585	+	// reconstructed session is still written and launched under the current cwd below.
586	+	fromCwd := plan.fromCwd
587	+	if fromCwd == "" {
588	+		fromCwd = cwd
589	+	}
590	+	fromSession, err := plan.from.GetAgentChatSession(fromCwd, plan.sessionID, false)
591	+	if err != nil {
592	+		return "", fmt.Errorf("failed to load source session: %w", err)
593	+	}
594	+	if fromSession == nil || fromSession.SessionData == nil {
595	+		return "", fmt.Errorf("source session %s has no data to reconstruct", plan.sessionID)
596	+	}
597	+
598	+	note := fmt.Sprintf("Resumed from a %s session via SpecStory.", plan.from.Name())
599	+	rec, err := plan.to.ReconstructSession(fromSession.SessionData, spi.ReconstructOptions{
600	+		WorkspaceRoot: cwd,
601	+		MigrationNote: note,
602	+	})
603	+	if err != nil {
604	+		if errors.Is(err, spi.ErrReconstructionUnsupported) {
605	+			return "", utils.ValidationError{Message: fmt.Sprintf(
606	+				"%s can't yet be a cross-agent resume target. Choose Claude Code or Codex CLI (or resume in %s itself).",
607	+				plan.to.Name(), plan.from.Name())}
608	+		}
609	+		return "", fmt.Errorf("failed to reconstruct session for %s: %w", plan.to.Name(), err)
610	+	}
611	+
612	+	path, err := plan.to.NativeSessionPath(cwd, rec.Filename)
613	+	if err != nil {
614	+		return "", fmt.Errorf("failed to resolve target session path: %w", err)
615	+	}
616	+	if err := writeReconstructedSession(path, rec.Content); err != nil {
617	+		return "", err
618	+	}
619	+	// The agent runs in a separate process and resolves the session by reading this file the
620	+	// instant it starts. On a filesystem without strong close-to-open coherence the freshly
621	+	// written file can lag, so confirm it is actually readable before handing the agent a
622	+	// --resume it would otherwise reject as "session not found".
623	+	if err := waitForSessionFileVisible(path, sessionFileVisibleTimeout); err != nil {
624	+		return "", fmt.Errorf("reconstructed %s session is not ready to resume: %w", plan.to.Name(), err)
625	+	}
626	+
627	+	slog.Info("resume: wrote reconstructed session", "path", path, "newID", rec.SessionID)
628	+	fprintf(out, "\nReconstructed %s session into %s as %s.\n", plan.from.Name(), plan.to.Name(), shortID(rec.SessionID))
629	+	return rec.SessionID, nil
630	+}
631	+
632	+// shortID abbreviates a session ID for display.
633	+func shortID(id string) string {
634	+	if len(id) <= 13 {
635	+		return id
636	+	}
637	+	return id[:5] + "..." + id[len(id)-5:]
638	+}
639	+
640	+// sessionFileVisibleTimeout bounds how long resume waits for a just-written reconstructed
641	+// session file to become readable to other processes before launching the agent. It is
642	+// generous enough to absorb the propagation lag of a weak-coherence filesystem (network or
643	+// roaming home, cloud-synced folder, VM/container shared mount) yet short enough not to hang
644	+// an interactive command — on a local disk the very first check passes, so it never waits.
645	+const sessionFileVisibleTimeout = 10 * time.Second
646	+
647	+// writeReconstructedSession durably and atomically writes a reconstructed session file. A
648	+// plain os.WriteFile leaves the data in the page cache and the new directory entry possibly
649	+// non-durable; on a filesystem without strong close-to-open coherence the separately-spawned
650	+// agent process can then fail to see a (large) file and report the session as missing. To
651	+// avoid that, the content is written to a temp file in the SAME directory (so the rename is
652	+// atomic rather than cross-device), fsync'd, renamed into place, and the parent directory is
653	+// fsync'd so the entry itself is durable.
654	+func writeReconstructedSession(path string, content []byte) (err error) {
655	+	dir := filepath.Dir(path)
656	+	if mkErr := os.MkdirAll(dir, 0o755); mkErr != nil {
657	+		return fmt.Errorf("failed to create target session directory: %w", mkErr)
658	+	}
659	+
660	+	tmp, err := os.CreateTemp(dir, ".resume-*.tmp")
661	+	if err != nil {
662	+		return fmt.Errorf("failed to create temp session file: %w", err)
663	+	}
664	+	tmpPath := tmp.Name()
665	+	// Best-effort cleanup of the temp file on any early return; a no-op once it is renamed away.
666	+	defer func() { _ = os.Remove(tmpPath) }()
667	+
668	+	// Preserve the world-readable mode the previous os.WriteFile(…, 0o644) produced
669	+	// (CreateTemp makes the file 0o600).
670	+	if chErr := tmp.Chmod(0o644); chErr != nil {
671	+		_ = tmp.Close()
672	+		return fmt.Errorf("failed to set session file mode: %w", chErr)
673	+	}
674	+	if _, wErr := tmp.Write(content); wErr != nil {
675	+		_ = tmp.Close()
676	+		return fmt.Errorf("failed to write temp session file: %w", wErr)
677	+	}
678	+	// Flush the bytes to the backing store before the rename so a reader cannot observe a
679	+	// renamed-but-empty file.
680	+	if syncErr := tmp.Sync(); syncErr != nil {
681	+		_ = tmp.Close()
682	+		return fmt.Errorf("failed to flush temp session file: %w", syncErr)
683	+	}
684	+	if closeErr := tmp.Close(); closeErr != nil {
685	+		return fmt.Errorf("failed to close temp session file: %w", closeErr)
686	+	}
687	+
688	+	if rnErr := os.Rename(tmpPath, path); rnErr != nil {
689	+		return fmt.Errorf("failed to move session file into place: %w", rnErr)
690	+	}
691	+
692	+	// Fsync the directory so the new entry is durable and propagates. Best-effort: not all
693	+	// platforms permit fsync on a directory handle, and it is a negligible cost on local disks.
694	+	if d, openErr := os.Open(dir); openErr == nil {
695	+		_ = d.Sync()
696	+		_ = d.Close()
697	+	}
698	+	return nil
699	+}
700	+
701	+// waitForSessionFileVisible polls until path is readable — present AND with at least its
702	+// first line available, since on a weak-coherence filesystem a stat can succeed before the
703	+// content propagates — or the timeout elapses, in which case it returns a diagnostic error
704	+// so the caller can fail clearly rather than launch a --resume the agent would reject.
705	+func waitForSessionFileVisible(path string, timeout time.Duration) error {
706	+	deadline := time.Now().Add(timeout)
707	+	for {
708	+		readable, checkErr := sessionFileReadable(path)
709	+		if readable {
710	+			return nil
711	+		}
712	+		if time.Now().After(deadline) {
713	+			return fmt.Errorf("session file %q did not become readable within %s "+
714	+				"(the target filesystem may lack close-to-open coherence): %w", path, timeout, checkErr)
715	+		}
716	+		time.Sleep(50 * time.Millisecond)
717	+	}
718	+}
719	+
720	+// sessionFileReadable reports whether path exists and at least its first line can be read.
721	+// A reconstructed session always has at least one record, so an empty read means the content
722	+// has not propagated yet even though the directory entry exists.
723	+func sessionFileReadable(path string) (bool, error) {
724	+	f, err := os.Open(path)
725	+	if err != nil {
726	+		return false, err
727	+	}
728	+	defer func() { _ = f.Close() }()
729	+
730	+	line, err := bufio.NewReader(f).ReadString('\n')
731	+	if len(line) > 0 {
732	+		return true, nil
733	+	}
734	+	if err == io.EOF || err == nil {
735	+		return false, fmt.Errorf("session file is empty")
736	+	}
737	+	return false, err
738	BODY:
739	`sessionFileReadable` assumes reconstructed sessions are newline-delimited text and uses `ReadString('\n')`. This breaks the visibility check for binary formats (e.g. Cursor's `store.db`) and can cause large reads/allocations while polling (it may read the entire file if no newline is encountered early). A safer readiness check is: can we open the file and read at least 1 byte?
740

744	LINE: 1058 START: 1054

746	@@ -959,3 +1019,192 @@ func extractCodexSessionMetadata(sessionInfo *codexSessionInfo) (*spi.SessionMet
747	 		Name:      name,
748	 	}, nil
749	 }
750	+
751	+// codexSessionHeader is the minimal metadata enumeration needs from a session file: the
752	+// session_meta fields plus the first user message (for slug/name), produced in a SINGLE pass.
753	+type codexSessionHeader struct {
754	+	sessionID        string
755	+	cwd              string
756	+	createdAt        string
757	+	firstUserMessage string
758	+}
759	+
760	+// userMessageMarker is the cheap substring screen for a Codex user_message record. Every real
761	+// user_message record contains it (it is the payload "type" value), so screening on it before a
762	+// full JSON parse never misses one; a rare false positive (the literal text inside an injected
763	+// context record) just costs one extra parse that codexUserMessageText then rejects.
764	+const userMessageMarker = `"user_message"`
765	+
766	+// scanCodexSessionHeader reads a Codex session file ONCE and extracts the session_meta
767	+// (id/cwd/timestamp from line 1) and the first user message. It deliberately avoids fully
768	+// parsing the large injected-context records (environment, instructions, AGENTS.md — often MB
769	+// each) that precede the first user message: each line is first cheaply screened for the
770	+// user_message marker, and only a candidate line is JSON-decoded. The result is identical to the
771	+// prior loadCodexSessionMeta + extractCodexSessionMetadata pair, in one pass instead of two opens.
772	+// Returns (nil, nil) for a session with no user message — treated as empty, as before.
773	+func scanCodexSessionHeader(sessionPath string) (*codexSessionHeader, error) {
774	+	file, err := os.Open(sessionPath)
775	+	if err != nil {
776	+		return nil, fmt.Errorf("failed to open session file: %w", err)
777	+	}
778	+	defer func() { _ = file.Close() }()
779	+
780	+	reader := bufio.NewReader(file)
781	+	h := &codexSessionHeader{}
782	+	lineNum := 0
783	+	for {
784	+		line, readErr := reader.ReadString('\n')
785	+		if readErr != nil && readErr != io.EOF {
786	+			return nil, fmt.Errorf("failed to read line: %w", readErr)
787	+		}
788	+		lineNum++
789	BODY:
790	`scanCodexSessionHeader` uses `ReadString('\n')` with no size guard. Codex context records can be very large, and this header scan runs in parallel; one pathological or huge line can cause large allocations/OOM even though the full parser path has `maxReasonableLineSize` protection. Add the same sanity limit here before trimming/parsing.

793	USER: Copilot
794	PATH: specstory-cli/pkg/utils/project_identity.go
795	LINE: 208 START: 190
796	DIFF_HUNK:
797	@@ -186,8 +181,30 @@ func (m *ProjectIdentityManager) generateWorkspaceID() string {
798	 	}
799	 
800	 	// The TypeScript version hashes the workspace URI, but in our case
801	-	// we'll hash the absolute path for compatibility
802	-	return m.createHash(absPath)
803	+	// we'll hash the absolute path for compatibility. The path is canonicalized first so
804	+	// the same physical directory yields one id regardless of the casing a caller passes
805	+	// (os.Getwd may echo a lowercase $PWD; a session's recorded cwd may differ in case).
806	+	return m.createHash(canonicalizeWorkspacePath(absPath))
807	+}
808	+
809	+// caseInsensitiveFilesystem reports whether the host OS treats paths case-insensitively.
810	+// macOS (APFS/HFS+) and Windows (NTFS) are case-insensitive/case-preserving; Linux is
811	+// case-sensitive. It is a var so tests can exercise both behaviors. Windows is included
812	+// even though the app is built only for macOS/Linux today: the in-flight Windows support
813	+// lives on a branch that does not carry this file, so folding case here keeps that branch
814	+// from silently reintroducing case-divergent workspace ids when it merges.
815	+var caseInsensitiveFilesystem = runtime.GOOS == "darwin" || runtime.GOOS == "windows"
816	+
817	+// canonicalizeWorkspacePath normalizes a path before it is hashed into a workspace_id. On
818	+// a case-insensitive filesystem the same directory can be named with different casing,
819	+// which would otherwise hash to different ids for one physical directory; case-folding
820	+// makes the id stable. On case-sensitive filesystems the path is left byte-exact, since
821	+// there two differently-cased paths are genuinely different directories.
822	+func canonicalizeWorkspacePath(path string) string {
823	+	if caseInsensitiveFilesystem {
824	+		return strings.ToLower(path)
825	+	}
826	+	return path
827	 }
828	BODY:
829	`caseInsensitiveFilesystem` is inferred from `runtime.GOOS`, but on macOS case-sensitivity is volume-dependent (APFS can be case-sensitive). Lowercasing paths unconditionally on darwin can incorrectly collapse two distinct directories on a case-sensitive volume into the same `workspace_id`. Consider detecting case sensitivity at runtime (best-effort) rather than assuming based on OS.
830

865	+// Best-effort UI writers. The destination is the user's terminal, so write
866	+// errors are not actionable and are intentionally ignored.
867	+func fprintf(w io.Writer, format string, a ...any) { _, _ = fmt.Fprintf(w, format, a...) }
868	+func fprintln(w io.Writer, a ...any)               { _, _ = fmt.Fprintln(w, a...) }
869	+func fprint(w io.Writer, a ...any)                 { _, _ = fmt.Fprint(w, a...) }
870	+
871	+// resumePlan captures the user's choices from the interactive selection.
872	+type resumePlan struct {
873	+	from      spi.Provider
874	+	fromID    string
875	+	sessionID string
876	+	// fromCwd is the directory the source session was originally launched from
877	+	// (the index's origin_cwd). It can differ from the user's current cwd when a
878	+	// session is picked from another project via the all-projects browser. The
879	+	// source data load must use this, because some providers (e.g. Codex) locate a
880	+	// session by matching its recorded cwd — looking under the current cwd would
881	+	// find nothing. Empty for older index rows; callers fall back to the current cwd.
882	+	fromCwd string
883	+	to      spi.Provider
884	+	toID    string
885	+}
886	+
887	+// agentChoice pairs a registry ID with its provider.
888	+type agentChoice struct {

890	+	provider spi.Provider
891	+}
892	+
893	+// CreateResumeCommand builds the `specstory resume` command. It opens the interactive
894	+// picker (a Bubble Tea TUI over the sessions.db index — see docs/RESUME-TUI.md): browse
895	+// the current project's sessions across all agents, pick one, choose a target agent, then
896	+// reconstruct (cross-agent) or native-resume (same agent) and launch with auto-save.
897	+func CreateResumeCommand(cloudURL *string, localTimeZone bool, debugDir string) *cobra.Command {
898	+	longDesc := `Resume a past coding-agent session — in the same agent, or a different one.
899	+
900	+'resume' opens an interactive picker of the sessions in the current project across all
901	+agents. Pick one, choose which installed agent to continue it in, and go. Resuming into a
902	+different agent reconstructs the conversation into that agent's native format first.
903	+
904	+Pass an agent to set the resume target up front, e.g. 'specstory resume codex' — the picker
905	+then skips the target-selection step and resumes straight into that agent. The agent must be
906	+a known, installed provider, or the command errors.`
907	+
908	+	resumeCmd := &cobra.Command{
909	+		Use:   "resume [agent]",
910	+		Short: "Resume a past session, optionally in a different agent",
911	+		Long:  longDesc,
912	+		Args:  cobra.MaximumNArgs(1),
913	+		RunE: func(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string) error {
914	+			config.EnsureDefaultProjectConfig()
915	+			slog.Info("Running in resume mode")
916	+
917	+			registry := factory.GetRegistry()

920	+			// An unknown agent name should fail the run rather than be silently ignored, so
921	+			// validate it against the registry up front. When set and valid, the picker skips
922	+			// the target-selection step entirely (see selectResumeViaTUI / beginResume); whether
923	+			// the named agent is actually installed is checked there, where the installed set
924	+			// is known.
925	+			presetTarget := ""
926	+			if len(args) == 1 {
927	+				presetTarget = strings.ToLower(strings.TrimSpace(args[0]))
928	+				if _, err := registry.Get(presetTarget); err != nil {
929	+					return utils.ValidationError{Message: fmt.Sprintf(
930	+						"unknown agent %q. Valid agents: %s", presetTarget, registry.GetProviderList())}
931	+				}
932	+			}
933	+
934	+			// Read flags (subset mirroring run/watch).
935	+			debugRaw, _ := cmd.Flags().GetBool("debug-raw")
936	+			useLocalTimezone, _ := cmd.Flags().GetBool("local-time-zone")
937	+			useUTC := !useLocalTimezone
938	+			outputDir, _ := cmd.Flags().GetString("output-dir")
939	+			flagDebugDir, _ := cmd.Flags().GetString("debug-dir")
940	+			noCloudSync, _ := cmd.Flags().GetBool("no-cloud-sync")
941	+			onlyCloudSync, _ := cmd.Flags().GetBool("only-cloud-sync")
942	+			provenanceEnabled, _ := cmd.Flags().GetBool("provenance")
943	+			noTelemetryPrompts, _ := cmd.Flags().GetBool("no-telemetry-prompts")
944	+
945	+			if flagDebugDir != "" {
946	+				spi.SetDebugBaseDir(flagDebugDir)
947	+			}
948	+
949	+			cwd, err := os.Getwd()
950	+			if err != nil {
951	+				slog.Error("Failed to get current working directory", "error", err)
952	+				return err
953	+			}
954	+
955	+			// Open (building if needed) the session index and run the interactive picker.
956	+			// builtFresh = the index was just rebuilt in the foreground, so the picker skips
957	+			// the background index warm (it would be redundant).
958	+			store, builtFresh, err := openOrBuildResumeIndex()
959	+			if err != nil {
960	+				return err
961	+			}
962	+			defer func() { _ = store.Close() }()
963	+
964	+			projectID, projectName, idErr := utils.ComputeProjectID(cwd)

967	+			}

970	+			if err != nil {
971	+				return err
972	+			}
973	+			if plan == nil {
974	+				// User quit or nothing to resume; the picker already explained why.
975	+				return nil
976	+			}
977	+
978	+			return launchResume(plan, cwd, resumeLaunchOpts{
979	+				outputDir:          outputDir,
980	+				flagDebugDir:       flagDebugDir,
981	+				debugRaw:           debugRaw,
982	+				useUTC:             useUTC,
983	+				noCloudSync:        noCloudSync,

985	+				provenanceEnabled:  provenanceEnabled,
986	+				noTelemetryPrompts: noTelemetryPrompts,
987	+			})
988	+		},
989	+	}

991	+	resumeCmd.Flags().String("output-dir", "", "custom output directory for markdown files (default: ./.specstory/history)")
992	+	resumeCmd.Flags().String("debug-dir", debugDir, "custom output directory for debug data (default: ./.specstory/debug)")

998	+	resumeCmd.Flags().Bool("provenance", false, "enable AI provenance tracking (correlate file changes to agent activity)")
999	+	_ = resumeCmd.Flags().MarkHidden("provenance")

1021	+// runs the agent with auto-save + provenance — the shared tail of `resume` and `search`.
1022	+func launchResume(plan *resumePlan, cwd string, o resumeLaunchOpts) error {
```

```text
1178	+// shortID abbreviates a session ID for display.
1179	+func shortID(id string) string {
1180	+	if len(id) <= 13 {
1181	+		return id
1182	+	}
1183	+	return id[:5] + "..." + id[len(id)-5:]
1184	+}
1185	+
1186	+// sessionFileVisibleTimeout bounds how long resume waits for a just-written reconstructed
1187	+// session file to become readable to other processes before launching the agent. It is
1188	+// generous enough to absorb the propagation lag of a weak-coherence filesystem (network or
1189	+// roaming home, cloud-synced folder, VM/container shared mount) yet short enough not to hang
1190	+// an interactive command — on a local disk the very first check passes, so it never waits.
1191	+const sessionFileVisibleTimeout = 10 * time.Second
1192	+
1193	+// writeReconstructedSession durably and atomically writes a reconstructed session file. A
1194	+// plain os.WriteFile leaves the data in the page cache and the new directory entry possibly
1195	+// non-durable; on a filesystem without strong close-to-open coherence the separately-spawned
1196	+// agent process can then fail to see a (large) file and report the session as missing. To
1197	+// avoid that, the content is written to a temp file in the SAME directory (so the rename is
1198	+// atomic rather than cross-device), fsync'd, renamed into place, and the parent directory is
1199	+// fsync'd so the entry itself is durable.
1200	+func writeReconstructedSession(path string, content []byte) (err error) {
1201	+	dir := filepath.Dir(path)
1202	+	if mkErr := os.MkdirAll(dir, 0o755); mkErr != nil {
1203	+		return fmt.Errorf("failed to create target session directory: %w", mkErr)
1204	+	}
1205	+
1206	+	tmp, err := os.CreateTemp(dir, ".resume-*.tmp")
1207	+	if err != nil {
1208	+		return fmt.Errorf("failed to create temp session file: %w", err)
1209	+	}
1210	+	tmpPath := tmp.Name()
1211	+	// Best-effort cleanup of the temp file on any early return; a no-op once it is renamed away.
1212	+	defer func() { _ = os.Remove(tmpPath) }()
1213	+
1214	+	// Preserve the world-readable mode the previous os.WriteFile(…, 0o644) produced
1215	+	// (CreateTemp makes the file 0o600).
1216	+	if chErr := tmp.Chmod(0o644); chErr != nil {
1217	+		_ = tmp.Close()
1218	+		return fmt.Errorf("failed to set session file mode: %w", chErr)
1219	+	}
1220	+	if _, wErr := tmp.Write(content); wErr != nil {
1221	+		_ = tmp.Close()
1222	+		return fmt.Errorf("failed to write temp session file: %w", wErr)
1223	+	}
1224	+	// Flush the bytes to the backing store before the rename so a reader cannot observe a
1225	+	// renamed-but-empty file.
1226	+	if syncErr := tmp.Sync(); syncErr != nil {
1227	+		_ = tmp.Close()
1228	+		return fmt.Errorf("failed to flush temp session file: %w", syncErr)
1229	+	}
1230	+	if closeErr := tmp.Close(); closeErr != nil {
1231	+		return fmt.Errorf("failed to close temp session file: %w", closeErr)
1232	+	}
1233	+
1234	+	if rnErr := os.Rename(tmpPath, path); rnErr != nil {
1235	+		return fmt.Errorf("failed to move session file into place: %w", rnErr)
1236	+	}
1237	+
1238	+	// Fsync the directory so the new entry is durable and propagates. Best-effort: not all
1239	+	// platforms permit fsync on a directory handle, and it is a negligible cost on local disks.
1240	+	if d, openErr := os.Open(dir); openErr == nil {
1241	+		_ = d.Sync()
1242	+		_ = d.Close()
1243	+	}
1244	+	return nil
1245	+}
1246	+
1247	+// waitForSessionFileVisible polls until path is readable — present AND with at least its
1248	+// first line available, since on a weak-coherence filesystem a stat can succeed before the
1249	+// content propagates — or the timeout elapses, in which case it returns a diagnostic error
1250	+// so the caller can fail clearly rather than launch a --resume the agent would reject.
1251	+func waitForSessionFileVisible(path string, timeout time.Duration) error {
1252	+	deadline := time.Now().Add(timeout)
1253	+	for {
1254	+		readable, checkErr := sessionFileReadable(path)
1255	+		if readable {
1256	+			return nil
1257	+		}
1258	+		if time.Now().After(deadline) {
1259	+			return fmt.Errorf("session file %q did not become readable within %s "+
1260	+				"(the target filesystem may lack close-to-open coherence): %w", path, timeout, checkErr)
1261	+		}
1262	+		time.Sleep(50 * time.Millisecond)
1263	+	}
1264	+}
1265	+
1266	+// sessionFileReadable reports whether path exists and at least its first line can be read.
1267	+// A reconstructed session always has at least one record, so an empty read means the content
1268	+// has not propagated yet even though the directory entry exists.
1269	+func sessionFileReadable(path string) (bool, error) {
1270	+	f, err := os.Open(path)
1271	+	if err != nil {
1272	+		return false, err
1273	+	}
1274	+	defer func() { _ = f.Close() }()
1275	+
1276	+	line, err := bufio.NewReader(f).ReadString('\n')
1277	+	if len(line) > 0 {
1278	+		return true, nil
1279	+	}
1280	+	if err == io.EOF || err == nil {
1281	+		return false, fmt.Errorf("session file is empty")
1282	+	}
1283	+	return false, err
1284	+}
1285	BODY:
1286	sessionFileReadable uses ReadString('\n') to decide whether a reconstructed session file is visible. This can read arbitrarily large data (until the first newline or EOF) and is especially risky for binary formats like Cursor's store.db, where it may scan a large portion of the file just to prove it's non-empty. Prefer reading a single byte so the visibility check is O(1) and format-agnostic.

1298	+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
1299	+// Walk-up identity resolution (shared by ComputeProjectID and the writer).
1300	+//
1301	+// The stored .specstory/.project.json fragments a monorepo: it is written per
1302	+// launch directory, and generateGitID looks for .git at exactly that directory and
1303	+// never walks up — so a session launched from a subdirectory gets no git_id and a
1304	+// path-based workspace_id all its own. The resolver below instead walks UP to the
1305	+// git root and computes identity from there, so every directory inside one repo
1306	+// resolves to a single project. See docs/SESSIONS-DB.md.
1307	+// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
1308	+
1309	+// originRemoteURLRegex captures the origin remote's url value from a git config file.
1310	+var originRemoteURLRegex = regexp.MustCompile(`\[remote "origin"\][^\[]*url\s*=\s*([^\n\r]+)`)
1311	+
1312	+// readOriginURL returns the origin remote URL from a git config file, or "" if the
1313	+// file is unreadable or has no origin remote.
1314	+func readOriginURL(gitConfigPath string) string {
1315	+	gitConfig, err := os.ReadFile(gitConfigPath)
1316	+	if err != nil {
1317	+		return ""
1318	+	}
1319	+	matches := originRemoteURLRegex.FindSubmatch(gitConfig)
1320	+	if len(matches) < 2 {
1321	+		return ""
1322	+	}
1323	+	return strings.TrimSpace(string(matches[1]))
1324	+}
1325	+
1326	+// findGitRoot walks up from dir to the nearest ancestor containing a .git entry and
1327	+// returns it. The .git entry may be a directory (normal clone) or a FILE (git
1328	+// worktree / submodule); both satisfy the search. Returns false if no .git is found
1329	+// before the filesystem root. The walk-up is what lets a session launched from a
1330	+// monorepo subdirectory resolve to the repo's identity.
1331	+func findGitRoot(dir string) (string, bool) {
1332	+	if abs, err := filepath.Abs(dir); err == nil {
1333	+		dir = abs
1334	+	}
1335	+	dir = filepath.Clean(dir)
1336	+	for {
1337	+		if _, err := os.Stat(filepath.Join(dir, ".git")); err == nil {
1338	+			return dir, true
1339	+		}
1340	+		parent := filepath.Dir(dir)
1341	+		if parent == dir {
1342	+			return "", false // reached the filesystem root without finding .git
1343	+		}
1344	+		dir = parent
1345	+	}
1346	+}
1347	+
1348	+// resolveGitConfigPath returns the path to the git config file that holds a root's
1349	+// remotes, handling both a normal `.git` directory and a `.git` FILE (worktrees /
1350	+// submodules, whose private gitdir points at a commondir that holds the shared
1351	+// config). Returns "" when it cannot be resolved.
1352	+func resolveGitConfigPath(gitRoot string) string {
1353	+	gitPath := filepath.Join(gitRoot, ".git")
1354	+	info, err := os.Stat(gitPath)
1355	+	if err != nil {
1356	+		return ""
1357	+	}
1358	+	if info.IsDir() {
1359	+		return filepath.Join(gitPath, "config")
1360	+	}
1361	+
1362	+	// `.git` is a file of the form "gitdir: <path>" pointing at the worktree's private
1363	+	// git dir; the shared config lives in the common dir, not that private dir.
1364	+	data, err := os.ReadFile(gitPath)
1365	+	if err != nil {
1366	+		return ""
1367	+	}
1368	+	content := strings.TrimSpace(string(data))
1369	+	if !strings.HasPrefix(content, "gitdir:") {
1370	+		return ""
1371	+	}
1372	+	gitDir := strings.TrimSpace(strings.TrimPrefix(content, "gitdir:"))
1373	+	if gitDir == "" {
1374	+		return ""
1375	+	}
1376	+	if !filepath.IsAbs(gitDir) {
1377	+		gitDir = filepath.Join(gitRoot, gitDir)
1378	+	}
1379	+	commonDir := gitDir
1380	+	if cd, err := os.ReadFile(filepath.Join(gitDir, "commondir")); err == nil {
1381	+		c := strings.TrimSpace(string(cd))
1382	+		if !filepath.IsAbs(c) {
1383	+			c = filepath.Join(gitDir, c)
1384	+		}
1385	+		commonDir = filepath.Clean(c)
1386	+	}
1387	+	return filepath.Join(commonDir, "config")
1388	+}
1389	+
1390	+// resolveIdentity computes a project's identity from a starting directory by walking
1391	+// up to the git root. It reads only git metadata — never any .specstory/.project.json
1392	+// — so it cannot be fooled by the per-directory identity files that fragment a
1393	+// monorepo. Returns the git_id (empty when there is no resolvable origin remote), the
1394	+// workspace_id (hash of the resolved root), the project name, and the resolved root.
1395	+func resolveIdentity(startDir string) (gitID, workspaceID, name, root string) {
1396	+	root = startDir
1397	+	if gr, ok := findGitRoot(startDir); ok {
1398	+		root = gr
1399	+	}
1400	+
1401	+	// A zero-state manager rooted at the resolved root reuses the existing hashing /
1402	+	// normalization / repo-name helpers (they do not depend on manager state).
1403	+	rooted := &ProjectIdentityManager{projectRoot: root}
1404	+	workspaceID = rooted.generateWorkspaceID()
1405	+	if cfg := resolveGitConfigPath(root); cfg != "" {
1406	+		if originURL := readOriginURL(cfg); originURL != "" {
1407	+			gitID = rooted.createHash(rooted.normalizeGitURL(originURL))
1408	+			name = rooted.parseGitRemoteURL(originURL)
1409	+		}
1410	+	}
1411	+	if name == "" {
1412	+		name = filepath.Base(root)
1413	+	}
1414	+	return gitID, workspaceID, name, root
1415	+}
1416	+
1417	+// ComputeProjectID resolves the restore-index project identity for a session's
1418	+// working directory. It walks up to the git root and computes identity fresh,
1419	+// ignoring any stored .specstory/.project.json (which fragments a monorepo) and
1420	+// never writing one. Returns the project id (git_id when a remote is resolvable,
1421	+// else the path-based workspace_id) and the project name.
1422	+func ComputeProjectID(cwd string) (id, name string, err error) {
1423	+	if strings.TrimSpace(cwd) == "" {
1424	+		return "", "", fmt.Errorf("cannot resolve project id: empty working directory")
1425	+	}
1426	+	gitID, workspaceID, name, _ := resolveIdentity(cwd)
1427	+
1428	+	// Prefer a resolvable git_id: it hashes the normalized remote URL, not a path, so it is
1429	+	// immune to the case divergence handled below and never fragments a monorepo.
1430	+	if gitID != "" {
1431	+		return gitID, name, nil
1432	+	}
1433	+
1434	+	// No git remote → use the path-based workspace_id. Prefer an id already persisted in the
1435	+	// launch dir's .project.json so every consumer (reindex, resume, cloud sync) agrees on a
1436	+	// single value, even for projects whose file predates path canonicalization. Only when no
1437	+	// file exists (e.g. a portable session whose recorded cwd is not present locally) do we
1438	+	// fall back to the freshly computed — and now canonical — hash.
1439	+	if stored := persistedWorkspaceID(cwd); stored != "" {
1440	+		return stored, name, nil
1441	+	}
1442	BODY:
1443	ComputeProjectID prefers a persisted workspace_id even when resolveIdentity walked up to a git root but couldn't resolve an origin remote. In a remote-less git monorepo, this can reintroduce subdir fragmentation if older subdirectories already have their own .specstory/.project.json with different workspace_ids. Consider only preferring a persisted workspace_id when no git root was found (i.e., root==cwd), and otherwise use the root-derived workspace hash so subdirs still collapse.

1469	BODY:
1470	caseInsensitiveFilesystem is derived from GOOS and the comment states macOS filesystems are case-insensitive. On macOS, APFS/HFS+ *can* be configured case-sensitive, so unconditional case-folding on darwin can collapse two distinct paths that differ only by case. If the OS-level heuristic is intentional for stability, the comment should reflect it's a best-effort default (and note the tradeoff) rather than a guaranteed filesystem property.

1523	scanCodexSessionHeader uses bufio.Reader.ReadString('\n'), which allocates the entire JSONL line into memory. Codex injected context records can be multi-MB; with parallel workers this can create large transient allocations (and potentially OOM) even though the code is trying to avoid parsing those records. Consider switching to a bounded read strategy (e.g., bufio.Scanner with an explicit max token size and a fallback that skips oversized lines while still searching for the user_message marker).
```

```text
1	package spi
2	
3	import (
4		"errors"
5		"strings"
6		"time"
7	
8		"github.com/specstoryai/getspecstory/specstory-cli/pkg/spi/schema"
9	)
10	
11	// ErrReconstructionUnsupported is returned by a provider that carries the
12	// ReconstructSession responsibility but does not yet have a native serializer.
13	// Reconstruction is on the Provider interface so every provider implements the
14	// method, but only some providers (initially Claude Code and Codex CLI) produce
15	// real native output.
16	var ErrReconstructionUnsupported = errors.New("session reconstruction not supported by this provider")
17	
18	// ReconstructOptions controls how a session is reconstructed into native form.
19	type ReconstructOptions struct {
20		// WorkspaceRoot is the target working directory the reconstructed session
21		// should belong to. Native paths/IDs are derived from it. When empty, the
22		// provider falls back to the SessionData's own WorkspaceRoot.
23		WorkspaceRoot string
24	
25		// MigrationNote, when set, is prepended as a leading agent turn so the agent
26		// understands the prior turns were imported from another session. Optional.
27		MigrationNote string
28	}
29	
30	// ReconstructedSession is the native output of reconstruction. It is data only;
31	// the caller decides where (and whether) to write it.
32	type ReconstructedSession struct {
33		SessionID string // freshly minted, native-format session ID
34		Filename  string // suggested native filename (base name, not a full path)
35		Content   []byte // native session file bytes
36	}
37	
38	// Turn is a single role-tagged text turn in the flattened transcript. The
39	// reconstruction model collapses everything an agent said, thought, or did into
40	// plain user/agent text turns (see docs/SESSION-PORTABILITY.md), which each
41	// provider then serializes into its native format.
42	type Turn struct {
43		Role string // schema.RoleUser or schema.RoleAgent
44		Text string
45	}
46	
47	// FlattenSessionData reduces a SessionData into an ordered list of plain
48	// user/agent text turns. This is the single place the cross-provider flattening
49	// policy lives, so every provider's serializer consumes the same transcript.
50	//
51	// Rules (per the locked design):
52	//   - user/agent text content -> a turn of that role
53	//   - thinking content -> folded into the agent turn's text (not dropped)
54	//   - tool calls -> folded into the agent turn's text via the pre-rendered
55	//     Summary / FormattedMarkdown captured on the forward pass
56	//   - model, usage, and path hints -> dropped
57	//
58	// migrationNote, when non-empty, becomes a leading agent turn.
59	func FlattenSessionData(data *schema.SessionData, migrationNote string) []Turn {
60		var turns []Turn
61	
62		if strings.TrimSpace(migrationNote) != "" {
63			turns = append(turns, Turn{Role: schema.RoleAgent, Text: strings.TrimSpace(migrationNote)})
64		}
65	
66		if data == nil {
67			return turns
68		}
69	
70		for i := range data.Exchanges {
71			for j := range data.Exchanges[i].Messages {
72				msg := &data.Exchanges[i].Messages[j]
73	
74				// Only user and agent roles produce turns; anything else is skipped.
75				if msg.Role != schema.RoleUser && msg.Role != schema.RoleAgent {
76					continue
77				}
78	
79				text := messageTurnText(msg)
80				if strings.TrimSpace(text) == "" {
81					continue
82				}
83	
84				// Drop synthetic, non-conversational turns (slash-command invocations,
85				// local-command output, etc.) so they are not replayed into the resumed
86				// session. This is intentionally reconstruction-only — archival markdown
87				// stays faithful to the source session.
88				if isSyntheticTurnText(text) {
89					continue
90				}
91	
92				turns = append(turns, Turn{Role: msg.Role, Text: text})
93			}
94		}
95	
96		return turns
97	}
98	
99	// PrepareTurns validates session data and flattens it into the neutral turn list
100	// every provider's serializer consumes. It centralizes the two guards each
101	// ReconstructSession must apply before it can build native output: the source
102	// SessionData must be non-nil, and flattening must yield at least one turn.
103	// Keeping these here means every provider rejects the same cases with the same
104	// messages and cannot drift apart.
105	func PrepareTurns(data *schema.SessionData, opts ReconstructOptions) ([]Turn, error) {
106		if data == nil {
107			return nil, errors.New("cannot reconstruct nil session data")
108		}
109	
110		turns := FlattenSessionData(data, opts.MigrationNote)
111		if len(turns) == 0 {
112			return nil, errors.New("session has no content to reconstruct")
113		}
114	
115		return turns, nil
116	}
117	
118	// ResolveWorkspaceRoot picks the working directory a reconstructed session
119	// belongs to: the explicit target from opts when set, otherwise the source
120	// session's own recorded root. Providers derive native paths/IDs from the
121	// result; the cursor provider is the lone exception that needs no workspace.
122	func ResolveWorkspaceRoot(opts ReconstructOptions, data *schema.SessionData) string {
123		if opts.WorkspaceRoot != "" {
124			return opts.WorkspaceRoot
125		}
126		if data == nil {
127			return ""
128		}
129		return data.WorkspaceRoot
130	}
131	
132	// reconstructMillisLayout is the RFC 3339 millisecond layout with a literal Z that the
133	// Claude Code, Codex, Droid, and Gemini serializers stamp on reconstructed records.
134	// (DeepSeek uses microsecond precision and formats its own.)
135	const reconstructMillisLayout = "2006-01-02T15:04:05.000Z"
136	
137	// RFC3339Millis formats t with millisecond precision and a literal Z, the timestamp shape
138	// shared by the Claude/Codex/Droid/Gemini serializers. Callers pass a UTC time.
139	func RFC3339Millis(t time.Time) string {
140		return t.Format(reconstructMillisLayout)
141	}
142	
143	// ResumedSessionTitle returns the display title for a reconstructed session: the trimmed
144	// source slug, or "Resumed session" when the slug is blank or whitespace-only. Centralizing
145	// this keeps the trim (which a couple of serializers previously skipped) and the fallback
146	// consistent across providers.
147	func ResumedSessionTitle(slug string) string {
148		if title := strings.TrimSpace(slug); title != "" {
149			return title
150		}
151		return "Resumed session"
152	}
153	
154	// ReconstructRole maps a flattened Turn role to the "user"/"assistant" role string used by
155	// most native session formats. Providers with a different role vocabulary (e.g. Gemini's
156	// "gemini", Codex's event labels) map their own.
157	func ReconstructRole(role string) string {
158		if role == schema.RoleUser {
159			return "user"
160		}
161		return "assistant"
162	}
163	
164	// SyntheticCommandTags are the wrapper tags coding agents inject for
165	// non-conversational slash-command records: the command invocation, its piped
166	// local stdout/stderr, and the local-command caveat. They are conversation
167	// scaffolding, never a real user prompt. This is the SINGLE SOURCE OF TRUTH for
168	// the tag set, shared by reconstruction's turn filter here (matched anywhere in a
169	// flattened turn) and Claude Code's title-selection filter
170	// (claudecode.isSyntheticMessage, matched as a leading tag), so the two cannot
171	// drift apart.
172	var SyntheticCommandTags = []string{
173		"<command-name>",
174		"<command-message>",
175		"<command-args>",
176		"<local-command-stdout>",
177		"<local-command-stderr>",
178		"<local-command-caveat>",
179	}
180	
181	// extraSyntheticTurnMarkers are synthetic markers beyond the shared command tags
182	// that must also never be replayed into a resumed session: Claude Code's
183	// <TEXTBLOCK> title-generation wrapper and its interrupt marker (which also has a
184	// "…for tool use" variant, caught by the prefix).
185	var extraSyntheticTurnMarkers = []string{
186		"<TEXTBLOCK>",
187		"[Request interrupted by user",
188	}
189	
190	// isSyntheticTurnText reports whether a flattened turn is a synthetic,
191	// non-conversational artifact that should not be replayed into a resumed session.
192	// It matches by Contains because a flattened turn concatenates content parts, so a
193	// marker can appear anywhere in the text. Markdown generation does NOT use this
194	// filter and stays faithful to source.
195	func isSyntheticTurnText(text string) bool {
196		for _, marker := range SyntheticCommandTags {
197			if strings.Contains(text, marker) {
198				return true
199			}
200		}
201		for _, marker := range extraSyntheticTurnMarkers {
202			if strings.Contains(text, marker) {
203				return true
204			}
205		}
206		return false
207	}
208	
209	// messageTurnText flattens a single message into its turn text: all text/thinking
210	// content parts joined, followed by the tool's pre-rendered markdown when present.
211	func messageTurnText(msg *schema.Message) string {
212		var parts []string
213	
214		for _, part := range msg.Content {
215			// Both "text" and "thinking" parts carry their content in Text; thinking
216			// is intentionally treated as ordinary agent text.
217			if strings.TrimSpace(part.Text) != "" {
218				parts = append(parts, part.Text)
219			}
220		}
221	
222		text := strings.Join(parts, "\n\n")
223	
224		if msg.Tool != nil {
225			toolText := toolTurnText(msg.Tool)
226			if toolText != "" {
227				if text != "" {
228					text += "\n\n" + toolText
229				} else {
230					text = toolText
231				}
232			}
233		}
234	
235		return text
236	}
237	
238	// toolTurnText renders a tool call as agent text, reusing the markdown the
239	// forward pass already produced (Summary + FormattedMarkdown). Falls back to a
240	// minimal description when neither is available.
241	func toolTurnText(tool *schema.ToolInfo) string {
242		var b strings.Builder
243	
244		if tool.Summary != nil && strings.TrimSpace(*tool.Summary) != "" {
245			b.WriteString(strings.TrimSpace(*tool.Summary))
246		}
247	
248		if tool.FormattedMarkdown != nil && strings.TrimSpace(*tool.FormattedMarkdown) != "" {
249			if b.Len() > 0 {
250				b.WriteString("\n\n")
251			}
252			b.WriteString(strings.TrimRight(*tool.FormattedMarkdown, "\n"))
253		}
254	
255		if b.Len() == 0 {
256			// Nothing pre-rendered; emit a minimal, non-empty description so the tool
257			// activity is at least acknowledged in the transcript.
258			b.WriteString("Tool use: " + tool.Name)
259		}
260	
261		return b.String()
262	}
263
```

## Finding 1 — `isSyntheticTurnText` matches markers with `Contains`, not a prefix

**File:** `pkg/spi/reconstruct.go` (Copilot saw it at ~L195–207)

### What Copilot is saying

`isSyntheticTurnText` drops any flattened turn whose text *contains* one of the synthetic wrapper markers (`<command-name>`, `<local-command-stdout>`, `<TEXTBLOCK>`, etc.) anywhere in the string. Copilot argues this is too aggressive: if a real user/agent message happens to mention one of these tags mid-sentence — or pastes code/prose containing it — the **entire turn is silently dropped** from the reconstructed (cross-agent resumed) session. It suggests a trimmed-**prefix** check instead, which would match "this turn *is* a wrapper record" rather than "this turn *mentions* a wrapper tag."

### Implication / ramifications

The blast radius is narrow and non-destructive:

- This filter runs **only on the reconstruction path** (cross-agent resume), never on archival markdown — the code and comments are explicit that markdown stays faithful to source. So the worst case is "a turn that should have been replayed into the new agent wasn't," not data loss.
- The dropped turn is gone from the *replayed transcript only*; the original session and its markdown are untouched.

So a false positive degrades a resumed conversation (a missing turn) but corrupts nothing.

### Why Copilot might be right

- The risk is genuinely reachable. `[Request interrupted by user` (added since the review) is close to natural language and could appear in pasted logs or quoted text. And `<command-name>`-style tags absolutely show up when someone is *discussing Claude Code internals* — this very conversation would be dropped.
- A `Contains` check has no upper bound on what it sweeps up; the intent really is "is this record a wrapper," which a prefix expresses more precisely.

### Why Copilot might be wrong

- **A plain prefix check would break the actual design.** The current code already documents why (L192–193): a flattened turn *concatenates* all content parts — text, thinking, and the tool's pre-rendered markdown — into one string. The synthetic marker is frequently **not** at the start; it's folded into the middle after other content. A `strings.HasPrefix` on the concatenated turn would *miss* the very records the filter exists to catch. Copilot reviewed an earlier snapshot and didn't have this concatenation context.
- These tags are distinctive XML-ish sentinels, not common substrings. Real-world false positives are rare outside the meta case of discussing the tags themselves.
- The code has since been refactored (single source of truth `SyntheticCommandTags`, an explicit comment justifying `Contains`), which suggests the author already considered and consciously rejected the prefix approach.

### My recommendation

**Keep `Contains`, but tighten where it's cheap and reduce the one genuinely fragile marker.** Specifically:

1. Leave the XML-style tag matching as `Contains` — a prefix check is wrong here for the concatenation reason above, and the false-positive risk for those tags is negligible.
2. The one marker I'd treat differently is `[Request interrupted by user` — it's the least tag-like and most likely to collide with quoted/pasted text. If we want to be conservative, the *right* place to filter is **before concatenation** — apply the synthetic check per content-part / per-message in `messageTurnText`, so we drop the synthetic *part* rather than nuking a whole turn that merely quotes a marker. That's the structurally correct version of what Copilot is gesturing at, without breaking the mid-turn matching.

If you'd rather not touch it, "won't fix — intentional, see the L190-194 comment" is a perfectly defensible reply to Copilot, because the comment already explains the design constraint Copilot missed.

The lowest-effort improvement that actually addresses the concern is option 2 (filter per-part before folding). It's a modest change to `messageTurnText`/`FlattenSessionData` and I can sketch it if you want.

```text
790	// their comments are untouched.

792		var block strings.Builder
```

## Finding 2 — `SaveResumePrefs` doc comment overclaims comment preservation

**File:** `pkg/config/config.go:759–762` (and `upsertResumeSection` at 787–842)

### What Copilot is saying

The `SaveResumePrefs` doc comment promises it "preserves comments so the self-documenting template stays intact," but `upsertResumeSection` **replaces the entire `[resume]` block** — including the explanatory comments *inside* that section — the first time prefs are saved. Either preserve those comments or fix the comment to match.

### Is it actually true? Yes.

I traced it:

- `upsertResumeSection` finds the `[resume]` line and rewrites everything from there "up to (but not including) the next top-level section" (L819–827).
- `[resume]` is the **last** section in the template, so there is no next section → `end = len(lines)` → the loop replaces the header *and all four comment lines under it* with just the active keys.
- On a fresh save you go from the commented, self-documenting block above to a bare:
  ```toml
  [resume]
  view_mode = "dense"
  ```

So Copilot's factual claim is correct: the `[resume]` section's own documentation is wiped on first save.

### Implication / ramifications

Very small. Two things to separate:

- **Other sections are genuinely untouched.** The walk-up/walk-to-next-section logic only rewrites the `[resume]` block, so the rest of the self-documenting template *does* survive. The `upsertResumeSection` doc comment (L787–790) is accurate.
- **Only the `[resume]` section's own 4 comment lines** disappear, and only once the user actually resumes. No data loss, no functional bug — purely the loss of inline documentation for one section the user has, by then, already started using.

### Why Copilot might be right

- The `SaveResumePrefs` prose literally says "the self-documenting template stays intact," and for the one section it touches, that's false. Comment-vs-behavior drift is exactly the kind of thing that misleads a future maintainer.

### Why Copilot might be wrong / why this is minor

- The *behavior* is fine and arguably correct. Preserving those comments would be awkward: the template's resume comments include **commented-out example keys** (`# view_mode = "sparse"`, `# last_agent = "claude"`). If you preserved them and then appended the active `view_mode = "dense"`, the file would show both a commented example and a live value for the same key — more confusing than the clean rewrite.
- It's a first-save-only cosmetic loss in a machine-managed section (`last_agent` is explicitly "managed automatically").

### My recommendation

**Fix the comment, not the code.** The cleanest resolution is to make the `SaveResumePrefs` doc comment honest and stop overclaiming:

- Change the promise from "preserving the rest of the file (comments and other sections), so the self-documenting template stays intact" to something like: "preserving **every other section and its comments**; the `[resume]` block itself is rewritten with the active keys (its template comments are replaced)."

That matches what the code actually does and what the already-accurate `upsertResumeSection` comment says. Trying to preserve the resume comments isn't worth the fiddliness and produces a messier file (duplicate commented/active keys).

This is a one-line doc fix. Want me to apply it, or move on to **Finding 3** (the `search` command saving the *initial* view mode instead of the user's toggled `rm.viewMode`)?

## Finding 3 — `search` persists the *initial* view mode, discarding the user's in-TUI toggle

**File:** `pkg/cmd/search.go:113`

### What Copilot is saying

When `specstory search` exits and resumes a hit, it saves prefs with `SaveResumePrefs(viewMode, …)` — where `viewMode` is the value **loaded from config at startup** (L74–78), not the value the user may have toggled with the `v` key during the TUI session. So if you open search in dense mode, press `v` to switch to sparse, then resume, the save writes `"dense"` back — silently reverting your latest choice.

### Is it true? Yes — and there's a correct reference implementation.

- `search.go:113`: `_ = config.SaveResumePrefs(viewMode, rm.result.targetID)` → uses the **stale local** `viewMode`.
- The TUI model tracks the live value in `rm.viewMode`, mutated by `toggleViewMode()` (session_tui.go:620–624) on the `v` key.
- The `resume` path does it **correctly** — `session_tui.go:1308`: `config.SaveResumePrefs(rm.viewMode, rm.result.targetID)` reads the toggled field off the returned model.

So `search` is simply inconsistent with `resume`. The model is right there (`rm`, asserted at L96); it just reads the wrong variable.

### Implication / ramifications

- Low severity, real annoyance. `view_mode` is a sticky user preference. A user who toggles to sparse in `search` and resumes has their preference quietly reset to whatever it was at launch. Toggling in `resume` sticks; toggling in `search` doesn't — confusing inconsistency.
- No data loss, no crash. Pure preference-persistence bug.
- Note the asymmetry: if the user *cancels* (L100–102 returns early), neither path saves — so the bug only bites when they actually resume from search.

### Why Copilot might be right

It's straightforwardly correct: `viewMode` and `rm.viewMode` can differ, and the saved one is the wrong one. The adjacent `resume` code proves what the intended behavior is.

### Why Copilot might be wrong

Only in severity framing — it's a minor UX regression, not a correctness/safety issue. But the diagnosis is accurate.

### My recommendation

**Fix it — one-token change** to match the `resume` path: use the toggled value from the returned model.

```go
_ = config.SaveResumePrefs(rm.viewMode, rm.result.targetID)
```

## Finding 4 — `caseInsensitiveFilesystem` assumes case-insensitivity from `runtime.GOOS`

**File:** `pkg/utils/project_identity.go:190–208` (4 duplicate Copilot comments)

### What Copilot is saying

`caseInsensitiveFilesystem = runtime.GOOS == "darwin" || runtime.GOOS == "windows"` decides case-folding purely from the OS. But on macOS, case-sensitivity is a **per-volume** property — APFS (and HFS+) can be formatted case-sensitive. On such a volume, unconditionally `strings.ToLower`-ing the path collapses two genuinely distinct directories (`…/Foo` vs `…/foo`) into one `workspace_id` → collisions / session mis-attribution. Suggestion: detect case-sensitivity at runtime (best-effort), or at minimum stop stating case-insensitivity as a guaranteed fact in the comment.

### Is the technical claim true? Yes.

APFS absolutely supports case-sensitive volumes (selectable at format time). The macOS *default* boot volume is case-insensitive, but "darwin ⇒ case-insensitive" is not guaranteed. The comment at L190–191 currently asserts it as fact.

### Implication / ramifications — and why the blast radius is narrow

The damage only materializes at the intersection of several rare conditions:

1. The user is on a **case-sensitive** macOS volume (uncommon — many Mac apps break on these, so the default is near-universal), **and**
2. They have two project directories differing **only** by case, **and**
3. Those projects have **no resolvable git remote** — because `workspace_id` is only the *fallback* identity. Per `ComputeProjectID` (Finding 7), a git remote produces a `git_id` from the normalized URL and the path is never hashed. So git-hosted projects are immune regardless of volume.

When all three hold: the two projects get the same `workspace_id`, so their sessions group together in the resume/search browser. It's **mis-grouping in the index — cosmetic, no data loss, no corruption.**

### Why Copilot might be right

- The factual claim is correct, and the comment overstates it as a filesystem guarantee.
- For the (rare) user on a case-sensitive APFS volume with case-divergent non-git projects, this is a genuine collision.

### Why Copilot might be wrong / why I'd resist the suggested fix

- **The OS heuristic is the deliberately-right tradeoff for the actual problem.** The bug this code *fixes* is the common one: on a case-**in**sensitive volume, `os.Getwd` may echo a lowercased `$PWD` while a session's recorded cwd has different casing — same physical dir, two ids. That hits ~all Mac users. Folding case solves it. The case-sensitive-volume regression is the rare inverse.
- **Runtime detection adds real cost and new failure modes** for a very rare case: you'd need per-volume probing (create/stat a file with flipped case, or a syscall), caching to avoid per-path I/O, and handling of network/roaming/container mounts where the probe is unreliable or racy (TOCTOU). That's a lot of surface area to defend against a corner most users will never hit — and it can introduce *its own* bugs that affect everyone.
- The author already made this call consciously — the comment even justifies including Windows for a future branch.

### My recommendation

**Keep the OS heuristic; fix the comment to stop asserting a guarantee.** This satisfies the two fallback comments and is the honest, low-risk move. Reword L190–196 to frame it as a best-effort default and name the accepted tradeoff — something like:

> `caseInsensitiveFilesystem` is a best-effort, OS-level default: macOS and Windows volumes are *usually* case-insensitive, Linux case-sensitive. It is intentionally not a per-volume probe. The trade it accepts: on a case-sensitive APFS volume, two directories differing only by case fold to one workspace_id. That is rare (case-sensitive macOS volumes are uncommon, and it only affects the path-based fallback id — git-remote projects hash the URL, not the path), and it is the deliberate inverse of the common bug this fixes: case-divergent references to the *same* dir on the usual case-insensitive volume.

I would **not** implement runtime per-volume detection unless you consider case-sensitive APFS a first-class supported configuration — the cost/benefit doesn't justify it.

Want me to apply that comment rewrite, or move to **Finding 5** (`sessionFileReadable` using `ReadString('\n')` — breaks for binary/Cursor `store.db`)?

```text
// waitForSessionFileVisible polls until path is readable — present AND with at least its
// first line available, since on a weak-coherence filesystem a stat can succeed before the
// content propagates — or the timeout elapses, in which case it returns a diagnostic error
// so the caller can fail clearly rather than launch a --resume the agent would reject.
func waitForSessionFileVisible(path string, timeout time.Duration) error {
	deadline := time.Now().Add(timeout)
	for {
		readable, checkErr := sessionFileReadable(path)
		if readable {
			return nil
		}
		if time.Now().After(deadline) {
			return fmt.Errorf("session file %q did not become readable within %s "+
				"(the target filesystem may lack close-to-open coherence): %w", path, timeout, checkErr)
		}
		time.Sleep(50 * time.Millisecond)
	}
}
```
</details></tool-use>

## Finding 5 — `sessionFileReadable` over-reads with `ReadString('\n')` (2 comments)

**File:** `pkg/cmd/resume.go:450–468`

### What Copilot is saying

`sessionFileReadable` is a readiness probe (does the just-written reconstructed session file exist and have content yet, polled every 50ms up to 10s). It uses `bufio.NewReader(f).ReadString('\n')`, which reads **until the first newline or EOF**. Two problems: (1) for a binary native format like Cursor's `store.db` there may be no early newline, so it can read a large chunk — or the whole file — into memory just to prove "non-empty," and it does this on every poll; (2) "first line" is a meaningless concept for binary. Copilot recommends reading **a single byte** so the check is O(1) and format-agnostic.

### Is it right? Yes — cleanly.

The function's actual contract is "is at least one byte readable" — note it already returns `true` on `len(line) > 0` **even without a newline** (ReadString returns the bytes-so-far alongside the error). So the newline scan buys *nothing* for the readiness decision; it only adds the over-read pathology. A one-byte read gives identical semantics for every case that matters:

- ≥1 byte readable → ready (same as now).
- 0 bytes / EOF → not ready, "empty" (same as now).
- real read error → propagate (same as now).

### Implication / ramifications

- **Today:** no live bug. Both reconstructed formats (Claude Code, Codex) are newline-delimited JSONL whose first line is small, so `ReadString` returns immediately. The risk is latent.
- **Tomorrow:** the memory notes a **Cursor reconstruct pass is planned**, and Cursor's native store is a binary `store.db`. The moment Cursor (or any binary format) becomes a reconstruction target, this probe could read megabytes per poll iteration during the visibility wait — wasteful and a potential large transient allocation.

So this is correct, low-risk, and genuinely future-proofing exactly the path the project is heading toward.

### Why Copilot might be wrong

Only a nuance: one could argue "first line present" is a *stronger* readiness signal than "1 byte present" on a weak-coherence FS. But it isn't, in practice — the write path (`writeReconstructedSession`) uses atomic rename + file `fsync` + dir `fsync`, so the file becomes visible whole, not byte-by-byte. A newline at byte 2 vs byte 2,000,000 tells you nothing extra about propagation. 1 byte is an equally valid proxy.

### My recommendation

**Apply the fix.** Replace the line read with a single-byte read, update the two doc comments that say "first line," and drop the now-unused `bufio` import. Let me do it:
